ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 22749003 represents the broader coffee beverage category; no specific latte code exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of hot latte is one 12 fl oz (approximately 360 g) cup, often served in cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee, latte, whole milk
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data for 'Coffee, latte, whole milk' directly from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID: 171127). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are not reported in this USDA entry; sentinel value -1 applied.

gi_source.title: Milk, full fat
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index estimated based on full-fat milk (GI 41) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as coffee itself has a negligible GI contribution. This assumes no added sugar.

hypertension.key_factor: Caffeine content and potential for added sugars.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor their caffeine intake and consider unsweetened lattes to manage blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Contains lactose (milk sugar) and often has added sugars, impacting blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Opt for unsweetened lattes and consider lower-fat or plant-based milk alternatives to manage carbohydrate and calorie intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Whole milk contributes saturated fat, which can impact cholesterol levels.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Choose lattes made with low-fat or skim milk to reduce saturated fat intake, and be mindful of overall dietary fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A hot latte is a popular coffee beverage made with espresso and steamed milk, topped with a thin layer of frothed milk. Originating from Italy, it is now a staple in cafes worldwide. The preparation involves brewing a shot of espresso, then steaming milk to a creamy texture, and finally pouring the milk into the espresso, creating a smooth, milky coffee. The taste is characterized by its rich coffee flavor mellowed by the sweetness and creaminess of milk, with a smooth, velvety texture. Nutritionally, a standard latte provides energy, protein, and calcium from the milk, along with caffeine from the coffee. It contains natural sugars (lactose) from milk, and its glycemic impact is generally low, depending on the type of milk and whether additional sugars or syrups are added. For individuals managing conditions like Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, choosing unsweetened versions and lower-fat milk options is advisable to control sugar and saturated fat intake.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is directly from USDA FoodData Central for 'Coffee, latte, whole milk' (FDC ID: 171127). GI data is based on full-fat milk from the University of Sydney GI Database, as coffee itself has negligible GI. Multilingual names are standard translations for a globally recognised beverage.
notes: This record assumes a standard hot latte made with whole milk and no added sugar. Nutritional values would vary significantly with different milk types (e.g., skim, oat, soy) or added sweeteners and flavorings. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for this food (FDC ID: 171127). polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for this food (FDC ID: 171127). trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for this food (FDC ID: 171127). dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as coffee with milk contains no dietary fibre. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the primary USDA source entry.
